meta_title: For Students: Git with AI to Solve can't debug code (Intermediate Resource Page)
meta_description: Learn how to Git with AI and overcome can't debug code. A Intermediate-level Resource Page using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: Master Debugging: Using Git with AI for Students
content.seo_description: Stop struggling with bugs. Learn how to use Git with AI, Antigravity, and Claude Code to debug faster and build a professional software engineering portfolio.
content.answer_block: Debugging isn't about guessing; it's about systematic isolation. By integrating Git with AI, you create a verifiable audit trail of state changes. Use Claude Code to analyze diffs and Antigravity to simulate edge cases, turning frustrating code blocks into clear, actionable refactoring tasks that accelerate your production velocity.
content.intro:
The screen glows, the cursor blinks, and your code refuses to compile. You have spent hours chasing a phantom error, feeling the weight of a project that seems to be falling apart. This is the 'debugging wall'—a rite of passage for every developer that separates the hobbyists from the engineers. 

But what if you could turn the tide? By treating your Git history as a diagnostic tool and pairing it with AI, you transform debugging from a guessing game into a surgical operation. You are about to learn the professional workflow that turns your repository into a powerful, self-correcting asset.

steps.title: Establish the Diagnostic Baseline
steps.description: Before fixing, you must isolate the failure. Use Git to identify the exact commit where the regression started.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Analyze the last 5 commits and identify the logic shift that introduced the current runtime error.
steps.claude_code_command: git bisect start; git bisect bad; git bisect good [commit_hash]

steps.title: Contextualize the Failure
steps.description: Feed the stack trace into Claude Code to map the error path against your codebase structure.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Map the stack trace to the relevant files and suggest the root cause based on recent dependency changes.

steps.title: Simulate and Verify Fixes
steps.description: Use Antigravity to run unit tests against the proposed fix without polluting your main branch.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a test suite that triggers the specific edge case causing the bug.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'generate unit tests to replicate bug'

steps.title: Atomic Commit Strategy
steps.description: Structure your fix into small, verifiable commits to ensure you can revert if the bug returns.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Review my current changes and suggest how to break them into atomic commits for better traceability.

steps.title: Automated Regression Prevention
steps.description: Integrate AI-driven checks to ensure the bug never reappears in future builds.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a CI/CD script snippet that flags this specific error pattern in future pull requests.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'create github action for regression testing'

pro_tips: Always commit your failing test case before you write the fix to prove the 'before' state.
Use 'git log -p' combined with AI analysis to see exactly how your mental model of the code diverged from the actual implementation.
Treat your Git history as a conversation with your future self; document 'why' you chose a specific fix, not just 'what' you changed.
When stuck, use AI to explain the code as if you were a junior developer to spot hidden assumptions.
Leverage Git branches for every experimental fix; never debug directly on the main development branch.
content.next_steps: Now that you have debugged your issue, push your changes to a new branch, open a Pull Request, and document your learning process in your portfolio README.
content.conversion_block: You are no longer just a student; you are a builder who understands how to navigate complex systems. Your ability to debug is your greatest career asset. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
